source_intake_record.raw_question_text: As in Fig. 3.46, $G$ is a ballistic galvanometer (i.e., one whose deflection $\theta$ is proportional to the charge $Q$ which quickly flows through it). The coil $L$ as shown is initially in a magnetic field $B_{0}=0$. Switch $S$ is then closed, current $I=1 \mathrm{amp}$ flows, and $G$ deflects $\theta_{1}=0.5$ radian and returns to rest. Then the coil is quickly moved into a magnetic field $B_{2}$, and $G$ is observed to deflect $\theta_{2}=1$ radian. What is the field $B_{2}$ (in any specified units)?

multi_step: solution likely combines at least two physical events: galvanometer kick when switch is closed and galvanometer kick when coil is moved into field B2
requires relating deflection to charge (theta proportional to Q) and then connecting charge to changing flux/current in the coil
numeric answer is not directly stated and must be inferred from comparing theta1=0.5 and theta2=1 with the apparatus parameters
question text is fairly long and condition-rich, suggesting multi-condition reasoning rather than single lookup
verifiability: answer is a short numeric text ('$63.4 \mathrm{~T}$'), which is easy to compare against an expected value
physics derivation appears rule-based from stated quantities (I=1 amp, theta1, theta2, B0=0), so downstream checking is feasible
asset registry passed and both question and answer are present, reducing uncertainty about missing data
answer_type=short_text supports exact or near-exact numeric verification

metadata.reasoning_chain:
Solution:
The direction of $\mathbf{B}_{2}$ is illustrated in Fig. 3.47.
Let the self-inductance of the coil $L$ be $L_{1}$, then
$$
\varepsilon_{1}=M  rac{d i_{2}}{d t}+L_{1}  rac{d i_{1}}{d t}
$$
or
$$
 rac{d q}{d t}=i_{1}= rac{\varepsilon_{1}}{R_{1}}= rac{M}{R_{1}}  rac{d i_{2}}{d t}+ rac{L_{1}}{R_{1}}  rac{d i_{1}}{d t},
$$
with
$$
i_{2}(0)=0, \quad i_{2}(\infty)=1 \mathrm{~A}, \quad i_{1}(0)=i_{1}(\infty)=0
$$

Integrating the circuit equation we obtain
$$
q_{1}=\int_{0}^{\infty}  rac{M}{R_{1}} d i_{2}+\int_{0}^{\infty}  rac{L_{1}}{R_{1}} d i_{1}= rac{M}{R_{1}} i_{2}(\infty) .
$$

When the coil is moved into the magnetic field $\mathbf{B}_{2}$, its induced emf is
$$
\varepsilon_{2}=- rac{d \psi}{d t}
$$
with
$$
\psi_{1}(\infty)=-N B_{2} S, \quad \psi_{1}(0)=0 .
$$

Thus
$$
 rac{d q_{2}}{d t}=i_{2}= rac{\varepsilon_{2}}{R_{1}}= rac{-1}{R_{1}}  rac{d \psi_{1}}{d t}
$$
giving
$$
q_{2}= rac{N B_{2} \pi a^{2}}{R_{1}} .
$$

As $q \propto 	heta$, we have
$$
 rac{	heta_{1}}{	heta_{2}}= rac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}= rac{M i_{2}(\infty)}{N B_{2} \pi a^{2}},
$$
or
$$
B_{2}= rac{	heta_{2} M i_{2}(\infty)}{	heta_{1} N \pi a^{2}}= rac{1 	imes 1 	imes 1}{0.5 	imes 100 	imes \pi 	imes 10^{-4}}=63.4 \mathrm{~T} .
$$
